articles.title: Updating my SSD's firmware fixed a chronic Windows problem that nothing else did
articles.author: Tanveer Singh
articles.content:
I've been using Windows 10 on my main PC since 2017, and I've been mostly satisfied, so much so that I plan to stick with it a little longer. That said, until last year, I couldn't get rid of OS corruption issues that kept recurring every few months. My SSD was obviously the first thing I suspected, but everything seemed to check out. The one thing I glossed over was the drive's firmware, which I had never thought of updating. When I finally did it, it fixed all my Windows corruption issues overnight. I haven't had to revert to a system image in well over a year. I suspect many of you have never updated your SSD's firmware, assuming it didn't need updating. While these updates can be rare, skipping them can be detrimental to your SSD's performance, stability, and data integrity, as I found out the hard way.
I had given up fixing frequent file corruption on Windows 10
The root cause eluded me for years
For about 2 years, my Windows 10 PC used to give me the dreaded black screen of death every 3–4 months. Nothing I did could fix the OS whenever this happened — not Startup Repair, not System Restore, not SFC and DISM scans. Until last year, my go-to solution was to use a week-old Macrium Reflect system image to revert the PC to a functioning state. I used to create weekly images on an automated schedule, and used this "fix" whenever the OS got corrupted.
Of course, I suspected that my SSD was faulty, although it was a new drive that I got with the new PC. Still, I ran a few tests to check the drive health, but found nothing noteworthy. I also ran a deep malware scan, and eventually installed a fresh copy of Windows 10 to eliminate the possibility of errors in the system image itself. Still, I was no closer to discovering the root cause of the frequent OS corruption. In a nutshell, I had resigned myself to the problem.
I updated the SSD firmware one fine day, which finally fixed the issue
I should have done it sooner
For close to three years, I never thought about updating my SSD's firmware. It isn't something most people think about, to be fair. Your GPU has driver updates, your OS has feature and security updates, and your motherboard has BIOS updates. Beyond that, it's easy to ignore things like SSD firmware, especially when everything seems to be running fine. In my case, things were clearly not fine, but my troubleshooting never suspected the SSD firmware.
Then, one day, out of the blue, I launched the WD Dashboard (now SanDisk Dashboard) to check the SSD's remaining lifespan. I was writing an article on the same, and was curious about how much of my SSD's lifespan I had used in three years. The answer was 1%, but what caught my eye was a notification at the top of the window alerting me that a new firmware was available. That's when I finally realized that I was missing a crucial piece of the puzzle. Updating the firmware was the only change I made to the SSD, and it got rid of the OS corruption issue. I haven't seen the black screen of death or any other major BSOD in over a year, so the new firmware must have fixed a bug in the data handling logic of the SSD. Given enough time, incorrectly written data can lead to system corruption, which is what I suspect was going on with my drive.
SSD firmware updates have been known to fix critical issues
The track record is far from spotless
Firmware sounds like a low-level microcode update that isn't as flashy as a major Windows update or a Game Ready Nvidia driver. However, it can greatly impact your SSD's day-to-day performance and reliability, while also ensuring data integrity. Everything from garbage collection and wear leveling to caching behavior and power states is governed by the SSD firmware. Any deficiencies or delayed updates mean your SSD is exposed to slow speeds, stability issues, and even file corruption.
SSD manufacturers often patch firmware bugs silently, so you may not know what changes a particular firmware will bring to your system. It's always recommended to update your SSD to the latest firmware if your drive's software has an update available. Checking for firmware updates every 4–6 months is all you need to ensure your SSD stays updated. Besides performance and stability improvements, some firmware versions even save your SSD from failure or at least prevent fatal errors.
For instance, firmware-linked drive failures were seen on Samsung's 980 Pro SSDs during the drive's early days. The older firmware was rapidly degrading the drives, causing them to fail prematurely. Samsung released a major firmware update that got rid of the issue and showed the impact that firmware can have on an SSD.
Another notable instance occurred on several WD SSDs when the Windows 11 24H2 update caused BSODs on PCs running these models. The new Windows update made changes to how SSDs handle the Host Memory Buffer (HMB), and before the firmware patch released by WD, some models were failing to update to the new Windows version. Users had to update their SSD firmware using the company's Dashboard tool to allow the 24H2 update to be installed. SSD failures were also reported on models with the Phison E16/E18 controllers during the Windows 11 24H2 rollout. Investigation revealed that these drives were running pre-release firmware instead of the stable retail version. This firmware behaved unpredictably under sustained workloads, and the 24H2 update possibly exacerbated the issue, bringing it to light.
Samsung 990 EVO Plus
- Storage capacity
- 1TB, 2TB, 4TB
- Hardware Interface
- PCIe Gen 4 x4 / Gen 5 x2
- TBW
- 600TB per TB
Stay on top of your SSD's firmware
You don't need to update your SSD firmware as frequently as your OS and drivers, but don't delay it for too long. Skipping crucial firmware versions can deprive your drive of performance improvements, stability fixes, and upgrades to background maintenance. On my system, a firmware update fixed a longstanding file corruption issue, and on many others, it saved the SSDs from BSODs and premature failure. The firmware is an integral part of your SSD, which shouldn't be ignored.
articles.summary:
Updating your SSD firmware can resolve persistent Windows file corruption and system stability issues that often go undiagnosed during standard troubleshooting of hardware and operating system errors.

### Key Points
- Outdated SSD firmware can cause recurring system crashes, file corruption, and "black screen of death" errors.
- Manufacturers like Western Digital and Samsung release firmware updates to patch critical bugs in data handling and power management.
- Tools such as the WD Dashboard or SanDisk Dashboard allow users to check drive health and identify available firmware updates.
- Firmware updates are essential for maintaining data integrity, as seen with fixes for Samsung 980 Pro degradation and Windows 11 24H2 compatibility issues.
- Experts recommend checking for SSD firmware updates every four to six months to ensure optimal performance and prevent premature drive failure.

### Why it Matters
Regularly updating SSD firmware is a critical maintenance step that ensures long-term hardware reliability and prevents data loss. Neglecting these updates can leave systems vulnerable to performance degradation and fatal errors that are often mistaken for software-based operating system corruption.

articles.title: I replaced these 4 popular Android apps with much better, lesser-known ones
articles.author: Abhijith N Arjunan
articles.content:
For the longest time on Android, I have relied on the Play Store to get my apps. Little did I realize at the beginning that the app recommendations were based on brand recognition, not the app's actual quality. As a result, my Android smartphones were filled with so-called popular apps from the Play Store. While these apps worked okay, they never quite lived up to expectations.
Fortunately, however, I got out of this popularity fever, and my productivity and privacy are thankful for that. These days, several lesser-known Android apps have replaced the most popular ones for me, and I am happy to recommend every one of them. Here are, however, the five best non-popular Android apps I can vouch for.
Ente Photos replaced Google Photos
It does everything Google Photos does, minus the privacy compromise.
Google Photos is undoubtedly the most popular app for managing and backing up photos on Android. Because the Photos app is tightly integrated with the core Android system, you get the benefits of performance and system-wide access. However, there's no denying that Google Photos is not the best option for privacy-conscious individuals. These compromises led me to try Ente Photos, which quickly replaced Google Photos for me.
Ente Photos is a privacy-first and ad-free photo backup solution. It allows you to save your photographic memories and share them with your loved ones without compromising privacy. The app does not use your data for sales or training; instead, it brings AI and machine learning to the device. So, as you do with Google Photos, you can search for people's photos based on their face, but without sharing this facial data with Google.
I also love how easy it is to set up and manage Ente Photos. It makes it impressively easy to import data from existing services and set up conditions for how backup and quality work. You can also use features like app lock and hidden photos that you don't find in Google Photos. The free version of this app gives you 10GB of storage, but you can expand it via paid plans. Overall, shifting to Ente Photos has been a much-awaited privacy upgrade.
Ente
- OS
- Windows, Mac, Android, IOS, Web
- Price model
- Free (paid plans available)
Ente is a browser-based photo gallery with a companion app that is similar in function and appearance to Google Photos. It is privacy-focused, making it a good alternative to many online photo galleries.
Next Player is better than VLC/MX Player
It proves that a cleaner, lighter app can do the job far more elegantly
I somehow knew better than to use the built-in video player on my Android from the very beginning. Coming from Windows, VLC was an obvious choice. However, over time, I felt that VLC was a little too clunky and not the most performance-friendly option either. Though I tried other popular options, like MX Player, the experience was subpar. It was rather recently that I came across Next Player, which has been my go-to media player on Android since.
The first thing I noticed about Next Player was its minimal user interface. It reminded me of the interface that MX Player had before it became an ad-filled nightmare. This ad-free app supports almost all media formats and offers additional features such as subtitles, picture-in-picture, gesture support, volume boost, controls lock, and media picker. It also offers multiple viewing modes to enjoy movies and TV series.
Next Player became so impressive that I forgot to install VLC media player on my recent Android devices. It makes it easy to manage large libraries of movies and TV series and ensures that I know what I am doing when I open an episode or a film. Considering that the app doesn't track you or collect any information, I cannot ask for more from this extraordinary alternative to VLC and MX Player.
Next Player
- OS
- Android
- Price model
- Free
Next Player is a modern Android video player with gesture controls, subtitle support, hardware acceleration, clean interface, and offline playback, designed for smooth performance without ads or unnecessary permissions requests.
AntennaPod outperforms Pocket Casts
No subscription, no tracking, no upsells
Pocket Casts (and briefly Spotify Podcasts) was my go-to podcast player on Android for the longest time. I didn't like the constant upselling behavior, but it was okay. However, the moment I came across the not-so-popular AntennaPod, I said goodbye to Pocket Casts. This completely free app offers more than I ask for from a podcast app, and it does so without constant subscription reminders, ads, or tracking.
This open-source podcast manager app for Android is the best way to access millions of free and paid podcasts across multiple platforms, including Apple Podcasts and the BBC. It makes it incredibly easy to find the podcast you are looking for and keep track of them, thanks to the multi-tab interface. I find it particularly impressive because it significantly reduces my cognitive load. The playback interface is also great, and you can use multiple gestures and control options to ensure the best experience.
I also noticed that the shift from Pocket Casts to AntennaPod did not make me miss any features, because the AntennaPod team does a great job of adding new features and listening to the community. It has since become my favorite way to listen to podcasts and learn more about them.
AntennaPod
- OS
- Android
- Price model
- Free
- Platform
- Google Play / F-Droid
Notesnook helped me avoid Keep & Evernote
Zero-knowledge encryption and a distraction-free interface offer a lot
Google Keep and Evernote were two apps I relied on heavily when I started using an Android smartphone. They remained trusted options for a while, but the limitations soon caught up. I had concerns about privacy. I did not feel comfortable sharing sensitive information with a tool that was so easily accessible and without encryption. Fortunately, I came across Notesnook, and it has been a secure space for my notes.
While not as popular as Google Keep or Evernote, Notesnook offers a no-spy note-taking experience that doesn't involve spying or tracking. Instead, all your notes remain encrypted, and you get multiple options to organize your notes and even a web clipper, for that matter. You can also connect your notes, so everything stays together. You also get support for reminders and rich content in notes via Markdown.
Notesnook has thus been an impressive upgrade over Keep and Evernote, without paying a dime. The free and open-source app offers an optional paid plan, but it does not limit core features on the free plan. So, it is an easy option to recommend for everyone who needs a better note-taking experience.
Notesnook
- OS
- Windows, macOS, Linux, Web, Android, iOS
- Price model
- Freemium with paid Pro plan
- Developer
- Streetwriters
These apps have a few things in common
You may have noticed that all these lesser-known Android apps offer a privacy-focused yet minimal experience. However, they do not compromise the core features for the sake of staying free or open-source. At a time when free apps are vanishing from the Play Store, it is nice to have such impressive alternatives without paying much.
I never realised how dependent I was on Google services until I tried to live without them. I've found some good alternatives that respect my privacy.
articles.summary:
Android users are increasingly turning to privacy-focused, ad-free alternatives like Ente Photos, Next Player, AntennaPod, and Notesnook to replace popular but data-heavy mainstream applications on their mobile devices.

### Key Points
- Ente Photos provides a secure, encrypted alternative to Google Photos with 10GB of free storage and on-device AI facial recognition.
- Next Player offers a lightweight, ad-free video playback experience with gesture controls and broad format support, replacing VLC and MX Player.
- AntennaPod is an open-source, tracker-free podcast manager that aggregates content from platforms like Apple Podcasts and the BBC without subscription fees.
- Notesnook serves as a secure, zero-knowledge encrypted note-taking application that functions as a private alternative to Google Keep and Evernote.
- These applications prioritize user privacy and minimal interfaces while maintaining core functionality without intrusive tracking or data monetization.

### Why it Matters
Transitioning to these privacy-centric alternatives allows users to regain control over their personal data while often improving overall device performance. By moving away from mainstream apps that rely on ad-supported models, users can enjoy feature-rich tools that prioritize security and user experience over data collection.

articles.title: Live: Fuel crisis set to worsen with massive fire at Geelong refinery
articles.author: Daniel Ziffer
articles.content:
Unemployment remains at 4.3 per cent, ASX closes lower — as it happened
The ASX has closed lower after the release of ABS labour force data showing the unemployment remains at 4.3 per cent in seasonally adjusted terms.
Meanwhile, a massive fire at one of Australia's two oil refineries was caused by equipment failure.
Viva Energy, which owns the Geelong refinery, is in a trading halt until at least Monday.
Look back on the day's financial news and insights from our specialist business reporters on our live blog.
Disclaimer: this blog is not intended as investment advice.
Key Events
Submit a comment or question
Live updates
Market snapshot
ASX 200: -0.3% at 8,955 points
Australian dollar: Flat at 72 US cents
S&P 500: +0.8% to 7,023 points
Nasdaq: +1.6% to 24,016 points
FTSE: -0.5% to 10,560 points
EuroStoxx: -0.4% to 617 points
Spot gold: +0.6% to $US4,817/ounce
Brent crude: +0.01% to $US94.14/barrel
Iron ore: +0.7% to $US106.60/tonne
Bitcoin: +0.25% to $US74,999.66
Prices current around 4:38pm AEST.
Live updates on the major ASX indices:
New: Filters
Choose what information you see below by using filters
That's all for today
That's all from us today for the markets blog.
Stay tuned on all the latest business and economics news on our website.
Have a great night. I'm off to the Melbourne International Comedy festival to forget about all the doom and gloom for a bit.
Key Event
Fuel spend falls after excise cut, consumers stockpile
Really interesting note here from NAB, based on the bank's transactions data to April 4, which includes the first few days of the halved excise tax.
It says fuel spending in the week to April 4 fell 14 per cent week on week, reflecting the fallback in prices following the halving of the excise tax from April 1.
"Spending remained 32 per cent above February levels," NAB senior economist Taylor Nugent said.
"The number of transactions remains 4 per cent higher than February, but also fell back, consistent with somewhat fewer refuelling 'top-ups' or precautionary purchases.
"In previous years, fuel transactions have fallen back the week following Easter but held up or increased a little in the lead up to the long weekend."
In terms of travel, airfare spending continues to be impacted by the timing of refunds and payments, while accommodation spending had consistently been tracking more than 7 per cent above year-ago levels from early 2025 until February.
"There was an initial increase in spending, likely as travel plans were adjusted in the initial two weeks of the conflict, but since then accommodation spending slowed to be no higher than in 2025," Mr Nugent said.
Some consumers were also stockpiling food.
"Supermarket and food retailing spend tracked a bit stronger through March, consistent with some households stockpiling non-perishable food items," he said.
"The spike in the week to April 4, is a similar magnitude to previous years as households increase food purchases ahead of the Easter long weekend."
Key Event
ASX closes lower
The S&P/ASX 200 closed lower Thursday, dropping 23.7 points or 0.26 per cent to 8,955 points.
The sharemarket fall came after the ABS release of labour force data showing the unemployment rate held steady at 4.3 per cent in March.
Economists still expect the Reserve Bank to hike interest rates in May, and say the jobs data pre-dates the recent sharp fall in confidence due to the Middle East oil price shock.
"We expect the jobs market to weaken in the coming months," HSBC chief economist Paul Bloxham said.
"Combined with our expectation of elevated core inflation in Q1, today's jobs market print supports our view that the RBA will lift its cash rate again in May."
He said the Reserve Bank board would face "very tricky" choices going forward.
"Inflation is already too high and is expected to rise sharply in the short run," he said.
"At the same time, sharply weaker sentiment suggests growth is set to weaken, albeit it is hard to know by how much."
Mr Bloxham said beyond the May meeting, the RBA's decisions will depend on how quickly the economy is weakening and whether there are signs that this is feeding through to significant weakening in the jobs market.
"At the point where the RBA believes the jobs market has turned decisively into a downturn, signalling that the unemployment rate is set to rise tangibly, we expect there will no further hikes," he said.
"In our central case we expect that there will be enough evidence of this by the RBA's June meeting.
"However, as is always the case when trying to forecast turning points, there is considerable uncertainty about this forecast."
ICYMI: Monday's video finance report
Did you miss Alan Kohler’s / David Chau’s finance report last night? Don’t stress, we’ve got you. The numbers refer to yesterday’s data, so catch up on where we’ve been, to get a steer on where you’re going today.
Analyst warns of the risks of a private credit crisis
#ICYMI ABC's David Taylor had an interesting discussion with founder and portfolio manager Dan Rasmussen of Verdad Advisers on The Business. Rasmussen says the multi‑trillion‑dollar private credit industry is in the early stages of a crisis, warning it could spread to the wider financial system.
Loading...The impact of the Viva energy refinery fire
It was a shocking scene for most Australians to wake up to. The Viva energy refinery ablaze.
The fire has raised further questions about Australia’s vulnerability to cuts in fuel supply. Could biofuels be part of the answer?
Carrington Clarke and ABC Business reporter Daniel Ziffer break it down in the latest episode of the ABC Business Daily podcast. Listen here:
Time will tell if impact of this oil shock is like those in the 1970s
Hi DC, The negative comments do not "intend to create panic". They are meant to draw attention to that most awkward of beasts - REALITY. I remember the 1973 oil shock - and it's nowhere near as significant as the current situation. Even then, the Straits of Hormuz remained open, and have done so ever since. The mere threat of closure has been enough to prevent all but the current senile moron falling into that trap. It took ten years to recover from 1973. How long do you think it will take this time?
- Stan
Thanks for the comment Stan.
I am not old enough to remember the 1970s oil price shock.
But one of the things I have taken away from my interviews with economists this week - many who were around at the time of those shocks and in key positions at the RBA and elsewhere - is that while things might get really really bad, central banks are aware of what's coming and, some argue, better equipped to manage risks than they were back then.
Only time will tell of course, but in the meantime, I'm stocking up at Costco :)
Key Event
ACOSS signals worry over job losses, calls for federal budget support
Lobby group ACOSS says it is concerned by the possibility of rising unemployment and risks from any economic downturn by the end of the year and wants the government to use the May budget to better support Australia's most vulnerable.
"While the unemployment rate remains at 4.3 per cent in today's figures, some major banks and other forecasters are speculating that, depending on global events, the unemployment rate could rapidly rise," ACOSS CEO Cassandra Goldie noted.
"With some scenarios suggesting we could face an increase to between 5 and 6.5 per cent by the end of this year if a downturn occurs.
"This kind of increase could equate to a rise in unemployment of between 100,000 and 300,000. If this were to occur, the important post-pandemic gains in employment for people would disappear."
She said if unemployment and inflation rise sharply at the same time, the people most affected "will be those living on the lowest incomes in our community".
ACOSS is calling for the government to use the federal budget to:
• target financial support to people on the lowest incomes through lifting income support payments such as JobSeeker and Youth Allowance to at least $600 per week
• Invest in reforming employment services, to provide people with real support to find suitable paid work
• Cap wholesale gas prices, further reduce health care costs, cap rent increases, and cover solar subsidies on consumers' power bills
Key Event
China's economy grows faster than expected despite Iran war
China's economy grew faster than expected in the first three months of the year, even as other countries feel energy supply disruptions from the conflict in the Middle East.
China's gross domestic product grew 5 per cent in the March quarter.
Beijing cut its annual economic growth target last month to a range of 4.5 per cent to 5 per cent — its lowest expansion goal since 1991.
Westpac's Head of International Economics Elliot Clarke noted that China's economy had "offered the world a well-timed boost".
"China's Q1 momentum was principally due to the strength of exporters and the investment being undertaken across the economy," he said.
"The trade surplus averaged $88 billion through Q1, broadly in line with the average of 2024 and 2025.
"Moreover, the narrowing of the surplus in March came as a result of a surge in imports which is likely to, at least, partly reverse in coming months as exports continue to grow."
He said the Middle East conflict is likely to "sustainably increase demand for green technology from China, opening up an opportunity for the trade surplus to reach new historic highs".
"This will likely help GDP growth edge closer to 5 per cent in 2026 and hold at or above 4.5 per cent in 2027," he said.
"Still, to sure up future growth prospects, authorities must action their intended support for households and incentivise additional domestically-focused investment by private and public entities.
"Without this broadening of the growth pulse, aggregate momentum will remain susceptible to slipping towards, or through, 4 per cent in coming years."
Steady unemployment in March the 'calm before the storm'
Westpac economist Ryan Wells has put out a note titled "Calm Before the Storm" cautioning against too much optimism from today's steady jobs numbers.
"It is too early to expect flow-on effects from the Middle East shock or from recent interest rate rises to be appearing in labour market measures. Instead, today's data provides us with a picture of the starting point for the labour market before these forces impact," he argues.
That starting point, at least, was one of strength.
"As we predicted, the trend for employment growth was turning more positive before the onset of the Middle East conflict, with annual growth (on a three-month average basis) lifting further from a trough of 1.0%yr in January, up to 1.5%yr in March," he observes.
"The lift in employment growth stems from last year's recovery in domestic demand, which was starting to see job gains broaden across consumer- and business-facing industries."
He says a key factor behind the stable jobless rate has been relatively fewer people looking for work.
"This does challenge the RBA's assessment that 'the labour market has tightened a little recently' — it now looks much clearer that lower unemployment rates around the turn of the year were a result of temporarily weaker participation, not a sustained re-tightening," he argues.
Wells warns that the full employment impacts from the current energy price and interest rate shocks won't be evident until much later this year and into next.
"While new hiring decisions may be quickly put on hold, businesses tend to be slower to shed staff, due to the difficulty and costs involved in securing and training new staff if the decision needs to be reversed (especially when tight labour markets mean skilled workers are in relatively short supply). This means the labour market tends to be a 'lagging indicator' of the economy, especially when entering a slowdown," he notes.
"Our current forecasts have the bulk of the labour market softening in the second half of this year, where the unemployment rate is expected to lift to a quarter-average of 4.9% and stay around that level over 2027 as interest rate rises impact."
Too much doom and gloom?
From what i have seen on these updates, 99% of them are negative and intend to create panic. Initially all about war and now when whole world is neglecting and moving forward , Australian economists are fear mongering at their level best. It feels like they are forcing country in to depression.
- DC
Hi DC, thanks for feedback. Of course we don't mean to be all doom and gloom here at the ABC.
But we are reporting what major CEOs and economists are telling us. I guess how bad things get economically, for Australia and the rest of the world, really depends on how long the war drags on.
In its latest World Economic Outlook report, the IMF has painted three scenarios that each get progressively worse.
In the first scenario, in which energy supply disruptions related to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz are short-lived and average energy prices this year only rise 19 per cent on last year's levels to average $US82 a barrel, the IMF expects global GDP growth of 3.1 per cent and inflation of 4.4 per cent.
So if you want to focus on the best case scenario, that's the IMF's best case.
But it gave two other scenarios that don't paint such a rosy picture.
In an "adverse scenario", where oil prices average $US100 a barrel in 2026 and $US75 a barrel in 2027, growth is expected to fall to 2.5 per cent globally over 2026, while inflation would rise to 5.4 per cent.
In the third "severe scenario", in which energy supply disruptions extend into next year and oil prices average $US110 a barrel this year and $US125 next, the IMF warns growth could slow to 2 per cent in 2026 and 2.2 per cent 2027, while inflation "would exceed 6 per cent".
I report on those latter two scenarios here:
Key Event
Ticketmaster owner Live Nation loses monopoly case in US
Live Nation and its subsidiary Ticketmaster had a harmful monopoly on big concert venues, a New York jury has found.
The civil case, initially brought by the US government accused the live events giant of blocking venues from using multiple ticket sellers in an attempt to stop competition.
The jury found that Ticketmaster had overcharged customers in 22 states potentially costing the company hundreds of millions of dollars in fines.
Read more here:
CBA's Matt Comyn says economic security trumps economic efficiency
During a chat with ABC's Ellen Fanning, CBA chief executive Matt Comyn also said that rising geopolitical risks mean economic security now matters more than efficiency, and that ahead of the May budget the government faces difficult choices.
"There are some choices that need to be made on the expenditure side. You can make a case for targeted relief. You can also make a case that every dollar, particularly from a deficit perspective, is a tax on future generations.
"So, you got to weigh that up really carefully. Clearly, productivity, which underpins the improvement and living standards in Australia is an important aspect.
"And I do think, I'm sure the government will be weighing up some of the areas that perhaps we need to be making some investments, in sovereign capability, greater storage, refining, we do need to be very thoughtful about that."
Key Event
CBA's Matt Comyn says Middle East war is hitting Aussie households and businesses
Commonwealth Bank chief executive Matt Comyn spoke to 612 ABC Brisbane Drive presenter, Ellen Fanning a little earlier.
When asked about the state of Australia's economy, he said the impact of the Middle East conflict has quickly transmitted through the economy, especially the price of oil which has hit sentiment across households and businesses, with sectors like agriculture and transport hit.
"It's really going to hinge on how long it takes for hostilities to cease, and beyond that, how long it will take for supply chains to be repaired.
"Oil was trading, at least yesterday, in the low 90s ($90 a barrel) and it would have been $60 a barrel before the conflict started. It's probably, realistically going to take a few months to come back down.
"We're not forecasting a recession. We believe the economy will slow. It's probably more likely that the RBA will increase interest rates in May, because clearly, it's going to have a short-term impact on inflation."
Mr Comyn later added that the RBA's May decision is "finely balanced" and that "a lot can happen" before the board meets.
Key Event
Santos avoids strike on executive pay vote
Oil and gas giant Santos has avoided being hit with a strike on the shareholder vote on its remuneration report at its annual general meeting in Adelaide, but the company went close to hitting the 25 per cent mark, which would have triggered a strike.
Chairman Keith Spence said 76.64 per cent voted in favour on the remuneration report resolution, with 22.96 per cent against. A small portion of votes are undecided and will be finalised when the vote is published on the ASX.
Market Forces chief executive Will van de Pol said 22.96 per cent was one of the biggest ever votes against an Australian oil and gas company over executive pay bonuses.
Santos suffered a strike against executive pay in 2022 when over a quarter rejected its remuneration plans.
“The board has avoided a formal strike by the skin of its teeth so substantial protest votes against Santos executive bonuses and director re-elections can't be ignored," Mr van de Pol said.
"Santos must get its head out of the sand and listen to major investor and community concerns over high-cost, high-risk new gas projects when the world has never been more motivated to shift away from volatile fossil fuels."
Key Event
Sequoia says it's cleared ASX hurdle for sale of InterPrac Financial Planning
Sequoia Financial Group will not need shareholder approval or re-compliance with ASX listing rules to proceed with the sale of InterPrac it said in a statement to the ASX today.
InterPrac representatives include Venture Egg Financial Planning, which had channelled the retirement savings of more than 6,000 Australians into the now collapsed First Guardian and Shield funds.
Earlier this month, the corporate regulator ASIC, announced it wants receivers to probe InterPrac Financial Planning's solvency and its proposed sale by ASX-listed parent Sequoia Financial.
Sequoia last month announced the sale of InterPrac to firm Conquest Investment Partners for just $50,000.
If ASIC is successful in its bid, which will require court approval, receivers will investigate InterPrac's financial position and its solvency, and will also probe whether the proposed sale to Conquest is "bona fide, fair and reasonable".
Sequoia had entered a trading halt on April 8. In a statement to the ASX at the time the company revealed chief executive Garry Crole had divulged details of the InterPrac sale to a journalist before informing the market.
"Information was provided to the Professional Planner by a telephone conversation with a journalist late in the afternoon of 20 March 2026," Sequoia said in the April 8 ASX statement.
The company said Mr Crole had revealed to the journalist that it was in negotiations to sell InterPrac to Conquest.
"The Board notes that the regrettable non-compliance with the Listing Rule 15.7 was unintentional and at a point in time when the company's shares were in a trading halt," Sequoia's statement to the ASX on April 8 said.
But in another statement to the ASX on April 10 Mr Crole said he had realised the journalist that he spoke to was not from Professional Planner but from another publication or news outlet, which he could not recall.
Last year I did an exclusive interview with Garry Crole on Venture Egg's financial advisers channelling people into the First Guardian and Shield investment schemes. You can watch here:
Private credit crisis could infect system
Great interview with founder and portfolio manager at Verdad Advisers, Dan Rasmussen by my colleague David Taylor.
Loading...Essentially, the multi-trillion-dollar private credit industry is in the throws of a crisis and analysts warn it could soon infect the broader monetary system.
Investors are pulling their money out of several US funds facing financial stress (known as redemptions) and those same funds have responded by limiting redemptions.
While the private lending space makes up a relatively small proportion of overall lending in the US, its capacity to create a major problem for the financial system represents a clear and present danger to the global economy.
"What you're seeing is that people are realising private credit was offering yields that were very high, much higher than you would have earned from safer fixed income instruments, and all of a sudden, investors in these high-yielding vehicles are learning that the marks are coming down, and that maybe they're not going to earn the very high yields they were promised."
Mr Rasmussen says the big difference between now and the financial crisis of 2008/2009 is that the holders of credit this time are private companies, rather than the public-traded banks.
However, the concern is that some of the holders of private credit are life insurance and retirement funds.
Key Event
Sydney auction clearance rate slumps below 50%
In another sign that Australia's two biggest property markets are now firmly into a downturn, Sydney's final auction clearance rate has dropped below 50%, while Melbourne's is heading towards that level.
Cotality reports that only 48.8% of properties auctioned in Sydney last week sold, down from 51.3% the week prior and 58.5% at the same time a year ago.
"This is the lowest result recorded since April 2020 when clearance rates crashed amid the onset of COVID," notes Cotality economist Annabelle Mezieres.
Melbourne didn't perform much better, with only 52.3% of auctioned properties selling under the hammer, down from 55% the week before and 62.2% last year.
Nationally, the capital city auction clearance rate fell to 52.7% last week, down from 54.2% the previous week and nearly 60% in the same week a year ago.
Clearance rates in the low 50s or below are historically correlated with falling prices.
Auctions are less prevalent outside Sydney and Melbourne, making this dataset most useful for analysing property trends in Australia's two biggest cities.
Auction numbers were up last week following the Easter break, but activity will ramp up again this week, with 2,618 homes scheduled for auction across the capital cities, up 40% on last week.
The Anzac Day long weekend in many states is expected to see auction numbers plunge back to fewer than 800 homes next week.
Key Event
RBA deputy says Australia's financial system not at risk from Middle East war
RBA deputy governor Andrew Hauser has been speaking again earlier today out of Washington DC, in a panel discussion about mid-term challenges for central banks.
On financial stability risks caused by the Iran war, he downplayed immediate concerns regarding the supply shock impact on Australian financial services, citing a high level of domestic resilience but pointed to his worries about global financial fragmentation.
He warned that receding cross-border cooperation could undo decades of progress in managing global contagion.
"Households have rebuilt their balance sheets over the course of the last few years. The banking system in Australia is almost comically well capitalised and highly liquid. We do have a large pension system, which are heavily invested in the US, and that issue, I suppose, could come to mind.
"I think the more substantial financial stability risk comes from this broad question of fragmentation where I think this is a big and growing issue.
"Increasingly separate forms of capital that find it hard to allocate across different countries, separate payment systems [inaudible] the regulatory competition and divergence, and what you are probably more likely to see, is sanctions, cross-border contagion and liquidity risk."

The Australian share market closed lower as the unemployment rate held steady at 4.3 per cent, while ongoing geopolitical tensions continue to pressure energy prices and economic sentiment.

### Key Points
- The S&P/ASX 200 index fell 0.26 per cent to close at 8,955 points.
